fact: Bob Pettit averaged 20.4 points and 17.0 rebounds per game for the St. Louis Hawks in the 1950s, winning two MVP awards and leading the franchise to its only NBA championship in 1958.
Lenny Wilkens ran the offense for the St. Louis and later Atlanta Hawks throughout the 1960s, averaging over 16 points and 6 assists per game while earning multiple All-Star selections.
Pete Maravich dazzled Atlanta fans with his no-look passes and creative scoring, averaging over 24 points per game across his seasons with the Hawks.
Dominique Wilkins, nicknamed The Human Highlight Film, won the NBA Slam Dunk Contest twice and averaged over 26 points per game for the Hawks throughout the 1980s.
Dikembe Mutombo brought elite shot-blocking to Atlanta, leading the NBA in blocks per game multiple times and earning Defensive Player of the Year while with the Hawks.
Josh Smith led the NBA in blocks in the 2006-07 season and was one of the most athletically gifted power forwards in the league during his time in Atlanta.
Al Horford anchored Atlanta's frontcourt for nearly a decade, making five All-Star teams and being the engine of the Hawks' 60-win season in 2014-15.
Trae Young became the first player since Nate Archibald in 1972-73 to finish in the top two in both scoring and assists in the same season, averaging 28.4 points and 9.4 assists in 2021-22.
Ed Sadowski averaged 19.4 points per game for the Celtics in the 1946-47 season, becoming one of the top scorers in the newly formed BAA.
Bob Cousy revolutionized the point guard position with his dribbling wizardry and no-look passes, winning the assists title eight consecutive seasons while leading the Celtics.
Bill Russell won five MVP awards during the 1960s, anchoring Boston's dynasty with his suffocating defense and rebounding and winning nine championships in the decade alone.
John Havlicek was the engine of the Celtics' 1974 and 1976 championship teams, earning Finals MVP in 1974 while being one of the most relentless two-way players in NBA history.
Larry Bird won three consecutive MVP awards from 1984 to 1986, becoming only the third player in NBA history to accomplish that feat while leading Boston to three championships.
Reggie Lewis was averaging 20.8 points per game in 1992-93 and blossoming into a genuine star before his tragic death at age 27 devastated the franchise.
Paul Pierce earned the 2008 Finals MVP after scoring 21.8 points per game in the postseason and willing Boston to its 17th championship over the Los Angeles Lakers.
Jayson Tatum made three All-Star teams in the 2010s and became the cornerstone of Boston's next dynasty, averaging 23.4 points per game in the 2019-20 season.
Jaylen Brown won the 2024 Finals MVP after averaging 21 points per game in the series, becoming the first Finals MVP in Celtics history whose team also had a teammate finish second in voting.
Julius Erving averaged 28.7 points per game for the New York Nets in the ABA, winning two championships and two Finals MVPs before the franchise joined the NBA in 1976.
Buck Williams was a relentless rebounder for the New Jersey Nets throughout the 1980s, averaging a double-double every season and earning Rookie of the Year in 1982.
Drazen Petrovic was shooting 51.7% from three-point range for New Jersey in 1992-93 and was on pace to become one of the greatest shooters in league history before his death in a car accident.
Jason Kidd orchestrated back-to-back Finals runs for the New Jersey Nets in 2002 and 2003, leading the league in assists and triple-doubles throughout his tenure.
Kevin Durant joined the Brooklyn Nets in 2019 and despite missing his first season to injury, quickly reminded the league he remained the most versatile scorer in the game.
Mikal Bridges averaged over 26 points per game in 2023-24, becoming the Nets' primary scorer after the superteam era collapsed and the franchise shifted toward a full rebuild.
Rex Chapman brought exciting scoring and athleticism to Charlotte's inaugural roster, averaging 16.9 points per game in his second season and giving the expansion Hornets a building block.
Larry Johnson won Rookie of the Year in 1992 and quickly became one of the most powerful forwards in the league, earning two All-Star selections while with the Hornets.
Emeka Okafor won Rookie of the Year with the Charlotte Bobcats in 2005, establishing himself as a defensive anchor who averaged 2.2 blocks per game in his debut season.
Kemba Walker became Charlotte's all-time leading scorer and earned three All-Star selections, putting up 25.6 points per game in 2018-19 on a non-playoff team.
LaMelo Ball won Rookie of the Year in 2021 and became the youngest player in NBA history to record a triple-double, showcasing a passing vision unlike anything seen from a teenager before.
Jerry Sloan embodied toughness for the early Bulls, becoming one of the league's premier defensive guards and earning six All-Defensive Team selections during his time in Chicago.
Bob Love was Chicago's most reliable scorer through much of the 1970s, making three All-Star appearances and averaging over 21 points per game across his Bulls career.

Michael Jordan averaged a then-record 37.1 points per game in 1986-87, becoming the second player in history to score 3,000 points in a season and announcing himself as the most dangerous scorer alive.
Michael Jordan won six Finals MVPs and six scoring titles in the 1990s, becoming the only player to win Finals MVP in every single Finals he appeared in.
Ben Gordon won Sixth Man of the Year in 2005 and was one of the most lethal instant-offense guards off the bench, routinely dropping 30-plus points in clutch situations for Chicago.
Derrick Rose won the MVP award in 2011 at just 22 years old, becoming the youngest MVP in NBA history after averaging 25.0 points, 7.7 assists, and 4.1 rebounds per game.
DeMar DeRozan hit a league-leading 11 buzzer-beaters in 2021-22, averaging 27.9 points per game and becoming one of the most clutch performers in the NBA in his first Bulls season.
Austin Carr was the first overall pick in 1971 and became a fan favorite in Cleveland, averaging over 21 points per game in his prime seasons as one of the franchise's first real stars.
Mark Price was one of the most accurate shooters of his era, shooting 90.4% from the free-throw line across his Cavaliers career and leading the team to four straight playoff appearances.
Brad Daugherty was a five-time All-Star who averaged a double-double for his entire Cavaliers career, establishing himself as one of the most underrated centers of the decade.
LeBron James scored 48 points in Game 5 of the 2007 Eastern Conference Finals, including the last 25 Cleveland points, single-handedly carrying the Cavaliers to their first Finals appearance.
LeBron James averaged a triple-double in the 2016 NBA Finals while leading a 3-1 comeback against the Warriors, delivering Cleveland its first championship in any major sport since 1964.
Donovan Mitchell scored 71 points against the Chicago Bulls in January 2023, the seventh-highest single-game total in NBA history, signaling that Cleveland had found their next franchise player.
Mark Aguirre was the Mavericks' first true star, making three All-Star appearances and averaging over 24 points per game in his best Dallas seasons throughout the 1980s.
Jamal Mashburn averaged 24.1 points per game in 1994-95, giving the rebuilding Mavericks a legitimate offensive threat and showing flashes of the star power the franchise needed.
Dirk Nowitzki won the 2007 MVP award as the first European player to earn the honor, then capped the decade by winning the 2011 Finals MVP after averaging 26 points per game against Miami.
Dirk Nowitzki played his entire 21-year career in Dallas, becoming the greatest European player in NBA history and retiring as the sixth all-time leading scorer with 31,560 career points.
Luka Doncic became the first player in NBA history to average at least 28 points, 8 rebounds, and 8 assists per game over a full season, accomplishing this in just his third year in the league.
David Thompson was one of the most explosive athletes the league had ever seen, averaging 27.2 points per game after the ABA-NBA merger and scoring 73 points in a single game in 1978.
Alex English was the NBA's leading scorer of the entire 1980s decade, totaling 19,682 points across the ten years and making eight consecutive All-Star appearances for Denver.
Dikembe Mutombo averaged 3.9 blocks per game in his Nuggets career and won Defensive Player of the Year in 1995, establishing himself as the most feared shot-blocker in the league.
Carmelo Anthony averaged 28.9 points per game in 2005-06, winning his first scoring title and announcing himself as one of the most unstoppable offensive players of his generation.
Nikola Jokic became the lowest-drafted player ever to win the MVP award, having been picked 41st overall in 2014 before redefining what a modern center could do with his playmaking.
Nikola Jokic became the first center to win three MVP awards since Bill Russell in 1965 and won the 2023 Finals MVP, completing one of the most dominant individual runs of the modern era.
Bobby McDermott was a standout guard for the Fort Wayne Pistons, earning multiple All-BAA honors and being one of the first players to popularize the two-handed set shot.
George Yardley became the first player in NBA history to score 2,000 points in a single season in 1957-58, averaging 27.8 points per game for the Fort Wayne and Detroit Pistons.
Dave DeBusschere was one of the most complete forwards of his era, earning multiple All-Defensive selections for Detroit before being traded to New York where he helped the Knicks win two titles.
Bob Lanier was an eight-time All-Star for Detroit, averaging 22.7 points and 11.2 rebounds per game across his Pistons career as one of the elite centers of the decade.
Isiah Thomas won two championships with the Bad Boy Pistons and earned the 1990 Finals MVP, cementing his legacy as one of the greatest point guards of all time despite standing just 6'1".
Grant Hill averaged 21.4 points and 8.4 assists in just his second season, earning five consecutive All-Star selections and being widely considered the next Michael Jordan upon entering the league.
Ben Wallace won four Defensive Player of the Year awards, becoming the shortest player in history to win the award at 6'9" and anchoring the 2004 championship defense.
Andre Drummond led the NBA in rebounding for four consecutive seasons with the Pistons, becoming one of the few players in history to average 14 or more rebounds per game.
Cade Cunningham was selected first overall in 2021 and quickly developed into one of the league's most complete point guards, averaging a near triple-double in the 2023-24 season.
Joe Fulks popularized the jump shot with the Philadelphia Warriors, winning the inaugural BAA scoring title with 23.2 points per game in 1946-47 and changing how the game was played forever.
Paul Arizin won two scoring titles for the Philadelphia Warriors, averaging 22.8 points per game for the decade and being the centerpiece of the franchise's 1956 NBA championship.
Wilt Chamberlain averaged 50.4 points and 25.7 rebounds per game for the Philadelphia Warriors in 1961-62, producing the most dominant individual season in NBA history.
Rick Barry led the Warriors to a sweep of the Washington Bullets in the 1975 Finals, averaging 29.5 points per game in the series and winning Finals MVP as a heavy underdog.
Chris Mullin was a pure shooter who made five All-Star teams with Golden State, consistently averaging over 20 points per game as one of the most reliable scorers in the Western Conference.
Tim Hardaway averaged 22.9 points and 10.0 assists per game in 1991-92 for Golden State, dribbling circles around defenders with his famous crossover as the engine of Run TMC.
Baron Davis engineered the upset of the top-seeded Dallas Mavericks in 2007, finishing off the series with 19 points and 8 assists in the clinching game and electrifying the Bay Area.
Stephen Curry became the first unanimous MVP in NBA history in 2016 after setting the single-season three-point record with 402 three-pointers, shattering his own previous record of 286.
Stephen Curry finally won his first Finals MVP award in 2022, averaging 31.2 points per game in the series to cap the dynasty he had built over more than a decade with the Warriors.
Pat Riley played for the San Diego Rockets in their first two NBA seasons, contributing as a young wing before going on to become one of the greatest coaches in NBA history.
Elvin Hayes averaged 21 points and 14 rebounds per game for the Houston Rockets in the 1970s, earning multiple All-Star selections as one of the most dominant big men of his era.
Hakeem Olajuwon won the Rookie of the Year award in 1985 and immediately established himself as the most skilled center in the league, averaging 20 points and 11 rebounds per game in the decade.
Hakeem Olajuwon won back-to-back championships and Finals MVPs in 1994 and 1995, with his Dream Shake post move considered the most unstoppable scoring technique of the entire decade.
Yao Ming became a global ambassador for the sport, earning eight All-Star selections and averaging 19.0 points and 9.2 rebounds per game despite his career being repeatedly interrupted by injuries.
James Harden won the 2018 MVP award after averaging 30.4 points per game and set the all-time record with 32 consecutive games scoring 30 or more points.
Alperen Sengun posted historic passing numbers for a center and earned his first All-Star selection in 2025, establishing himself as one of the most skilled young big men in the league.
Roger Brown was one of the ABA's biggest stars for the Pacers, averaging over 20 points per game and being a key piece of three ABA championships as one of the most complete forwards of his era.
Mel Daniels was a two-time ABA MVP for Indiana and remained with the franchise after the merger, regarded as the greatest center in ABA history during his time with the Pacers.
Reggie Miller was drafted in 1987 and immediately showed the shooting touch that would make him one of the deadliest three-point shooters and greatest clutch performers in NBA history.
Reggie Miller scored 8 points in 8.9 seconds against the New York Knicks in the 1995 playoffs, completing one of the most stunning clutch performances in NBA postseason history.
Jermaine O'Neal was a four-time All-Star for Indiana, averaging 20.1 points and 10.3 rebounds per game and anchoring one of the Eastern Conference's most dominant frontcourts.
Paul George won the Defensive Player of the Year award in 2014 and made six All-Star teams during his Pacers tenure, becoming one of the most complete two-way wings in the league.
Tyrese Haliburton led the NBA in assists in both 2023 and 2024, becoming the first player since John Stockton to lead the league in assists in multiple consecutive seasons.
Bob McAdoo won three consecutive scoring titles for the Buffalo Braves in the early 1970s, averaging 34.5 points per game in 1974-75 and winning the MVP award that year.
World B. Free was one of the most creative scorers on the San Diego and Los Angeles Clippers, at one point legally changing his first name to World to reflect his larger-than-life personality.
Danny Manning was a two-time All-Star for the Clippers, providing a consistent scoring presence in the post as one of the few bright spots on otherwise struggling Clippers rosters.
Elton Brand was a two-time All-Star for the Clippers, averaging 20.1 points and 10.0 rebounds per game and giving the franchise a genuine star during the most competitive era in team history.
Chris Paul ran the Clippers' offense with surgical precision, leading the NBA in assists four times during his tenure and dragging the franchise to its most sustained period of playoff success.
Kawhi Leonard hit a series-winning buzzer-beater against the Dallas Mavericks in the 2021 playoffs, then led the Clippers to the Western Conference Finals in one of the great individual playoff runs.
George Mikan was so dominant for the Minneapolis Lakers that the NBA widened the lane from 6 to 12 feet specifically to limit his effectiveness, and he still led the league in scoring.
Elgin Baylor joined the Lakers in 1958 and immediately averaged 24.9 points and 15.0 rebounds per game, becoming one of the most explosive and acrobatic scorers the league had ever seen.
Jerry West averaged 30.8 points per game in the 1965-66 season for the Lakers and remains the only player in NBA history to win Finals MVP while playing for the losing team.
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar joined the Lakers in 1975 and immediately made them contenders again, winning his fourth MVP award in 1976 and bringing his unstoppable skyhook to Los Angeles.
Magic Johnson averaged a career-best 23.9 points and 12.2 assists per game in 1986-87, leading the Showtime Lakers to the championship and winning the first of his three regular-season MVP awards.
Shaquille O'Neal joined the Lakers in 1996 and immediately became the most physically dominant player in the league, setting the stage for the three-peat dynasty that would follow in the 2000s.
Kobe Bryant scored 81 points against the Toronto Raptors on January 22, 2006, the second-highest single-game total in NBA history and the most iconic individual performance of the decade.
Kobe Bryant scored 60 points in the final game of his career in 2016, going out on his own terms in front of a sold-out Staples Center crowd in one of sport's most celebrated farewells.
LeBron James became the NBA's all-time leading scorer in 2023, surpassing Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's record of 38,387 points in a game against the Oklahoma City Thunder.
Shareef Abdur-Rahim averaged 18.8 points per game for the Vancouver Grizzlies and earned an All-Star selection in 2002, being the franchise's first genuine star player.
Pau Gasol won Rookie of the Year in 2002 and developed into a versatile All-Star, averaging 20 points and 9 rebounds per game and becoming the face of the Memphis franchise.
Marc Gasol won the Defensive Player of the Year award in 2013 and earned three All-Star selections as the anchor of the Grit and Grind era Memphis teams.
Ja Morant won Rookie of the Year in 2020 and led the Grizzlies to a 56-win season in 2021-22, becoming one of the most breathtaking athletes in the league with his explosive drives to the rim.
Rony Seikaly was drafted as the Heat's first-ever pick in 1988 and developed into a reliable double-double center, becoming the first notable big man in franchise history.
Alonzo Mourning won two Defensive Player of the Year awards for Miami, averaging 3.9 blocks per game in 1998-99 and serving as the defensive cornerstone of Pat Riley's physical Heat teams.
Dwyane Wade averaged 34.7 points per game in the 2006 NBA Finals, single-handedly willing Miami to its first championship over the Dallas Mavericks and earning Finals MVP.
LeBron James won two championships and two Finals MVPs in Miami, averaging 26.9 points, 7.8 rebounds, and 6.4 assists per game across his four seasons with the Heat.
Jimmy Butler averaged 35.0 points per game over four games of the 2023 Finals, dragging the eighth-seeded Heat to Game 6 against the Nuggets almost entirely on the strength of his will.
Jon McGlocklin was a sharpshooting guard who was one of the Bucks' original stars, providing perimeter scoring and helping establish the young franchise's offensive identity.
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar won the MVP award in his first three eligible seasons with the Bucks, averaging 31.7 points and 16.8 rebounds per game and winning Milwaukee its only championship in 1971.
Marques Johnson was a five-time All-Star for Milwaukee, averaging 20.1 points per game and being one of the most versatile small forwards in the Western Conference throughout the decade.
Ray Allen arrived in Milwaukee via trade in 1996 and quickly became one of the purest shooters in the league, earning two All-Star selections and making the Bucks a playoff team again.
Sam Cassell delivered a clutch performance in the 2001 Eastern Conference Finals, nearly lifting the Bucks past the Allen Iverson-led 76ers in a seven-game series that went down to the wire.
Giannis Antetokounmpo won back-to-back MVP awards in 2019 and 2020, becoming only the third player in NBA history to win consecutive MVPs since LeBron James did so in 2013.

Giannis Antetokounmpo dropped 50 points and 14 rebounds in the clinching Game 6 of the 2021 Finals, delivering the greatest individual performance in a championship-clinching game in modern NBA history.

Pooh Richardson averaged 17.6 points and 9.5 assists per game in 1990-91, becoming the Timberwolves' first legitimate playmaking point guard and establishing a floor general identity for the franchise.
Kevin Garnett averaged 18.5 points, 9.6 rebounds, and 4.3 assists per game across the 1990s for Minnesota, establishing himself as the most versatile big man of his generation.
Kevin Garnett won the MVP award in 2004 after averaging 24.2 points, 13.9 rebounds, and 5.0 assists per game, leading Minnesota to the Western Conference Finals in the franchise's best-ever run.
Karl-Anthony Towns won Rookie of the Year in 2016 and became the first player in NBA history to shoot above 50% from the field, 40% from three, and 80% from the line in multiple seasons.
Anthony Edwards emerged as one of the most electric players in the league, averaging over 25 points per game and leading the Timberwolves to their first Western Conference Finals since 2004.

Baron Davis averaged 22.7 points and 7.9 assists per game for the New Orleans Hornets in 2004-05, putting up one of the most complete guard seasons in team history before Hurricane Katrina displaced the franchise.
Anthony Davis won the scoring title in 2017-18, averaging 28.1 points per game, and earned six All-Star selections as a Pelican before requesting his trade to the Los Angeles Lakers.
Zion Williamson averaged 26.0 points per game while shooting 60.8% from the field in 2021-22, becoming the first player to average 25 points on over 60% shooting since Shaquille O'Neal.
Carl Braun was the Knicks' first great scorer, averaging 14.4 points per game across his career and making the All-Star team five times as the face of the early New York franchise.
Harry Gallatin set the then-record for consecutive games played at 682 while being one of the most reliable big men in the Eastern Division throughout the decade.
Willis Reed established himself as the cornerstone of the franchise during the 1960s, anchoring the defense and scoring in the post as the Knicks built toward their back-to-back championship core.

Walt Frazier averaged 19.7 points and 6.9 assists per game for the Knicks and won two championships, being one of the premier two-way guards of his era and one of the coolest dressers in sports history.
Patrick Ewing was drafted first overall in 1985 and immediately became one of the most dominant centers in the league, averaging 24.0 points and 11.2 rebounds per game in his rookie season.
John Starks hit a dunk over Michael Jordan and Horace Grant in the 1993 playoffs, becoming a symbol of the Knicks' fighting spirit in one of the most celebrated moments in franchise history.
Stephon Marbury was one of the quickest guards in the league during his Knicks tenure, routinely putting up 20-plus points and 8-plus assists per game despite the franchise struggling to win.
Carmelo Anthony won the NBA scoring title in 2012-13, averaging 28.7 points per game and becoming the first Knick to win the scoring title since Bernard King in 1984-85.
Jalen Brunson averaged 28.7 points and 6.7 assists per game in 2023-24, earning his first All-Star selection and becoming the most important Knicks player since Carmelo Anthony.

Bob Rule averaged 18 points per game for the Seattle SuperSonics in their early seasons, becoming one of the first reliable scorers in franchise history and giving the expansion team an offensive identity.
Dennis Johnson was the 1979 Finals MVP for the Seattle SuperSonics, averaging 22.6 points per game in the series and proving himself as one of the great two-way guards of his era.
Xavier McDaniel was a physical, high-scoring forward for Seattle in the late 1980s, earning an All-Star selection and being one of the most feared scorers in the Western Conference.
Gary Payton won the Defensive Player of the Year award in 1996 and led the Sonics to the Finals, becoming one of the most suffocating perimeter defenders in NBA history during his time in Seattle.

Kevin Durant was drafted second overall by Seattle in 2007 and won Rookie of the Year, then moved with the franchise to Oklahoma City where he became one of the most prolific scorers in league history.

Russell Westbrook won the MVP award in 2017 after averaging a triple-double for an entire season — 31.6 points, 10.7 rebounds, and 10.4 assists — the first player to do so since Oscar Robertson in 1962.
Shai Gilgeous-Alexander won the MVP award in 2025 after averaging 32.7 points per game in the regular season, then led the Thunder to the NBA championship with a series of dominant performances.

Scott Skiles set the NBA single-game assist record with 30 assists on December 30, 1990, a mark that still stands today and gave the young Magic franchise one of the most memorable records in league history.
Shaquille O'Neal was drafted first overall in 1992 and immediately became the most physically dominant player in the league, averaging 29.3 points and 13.2 rebounds in his final Orlando season.

Dwight Howard won three consecutive Defensive Player of the Year awards from 2009 to 2011, anchoring the Magic's run to the 2009 Finals and becoming the most dominant defensive center since Hakeem Olajuwon.
Nikola Vucevic was a two-time All-Star for Orlando, averaging a consistent double-double and being the steadiest player on the roster through years of rebuilding.
Paolo Banchero won Rookie of the Year in 2023 after averaging 20.0 points per game, becoming the first Magic player to average 20 points per game as a rookie since Shaquille O'Neal in 1992.

Dolph Schayes was one of the early stars of the Syracuse Nationals, combining scoring and rebounding in a way that foreshadowed the modern power forward and becoming one of the first 15,000-point scorers in history.
Dolph Schayes won the NBA scoring title in 1957-58 and was the first player in NBA history to surpass 15,000 career points, setting a milestone that proved his sustained dominance over the decade.
Wilt Chamberlain averaged 50.4 points and 25.7 rebounds for the Philadelphia Warriors in 1961-62 before joining the 76ers, posting the greatest individual statistical season in NBA history.
Julius Erving joined the 76ers in 1976 and immediately became the most electrifying player in the NBA, elevating above-the-rim basketball to a new level with his acrobatic dunks and scoring.
Moses Malone delivered on his promise of Fo, Fo, Fo in the 1983 playoffs, nearly going undefeated as the 76ers swept the Finals and Malone won his third MVP award.
Allen Iverson was drafted first overall in 1996 and immediately became the most electrifying player of his era, crossing over Michael Jordan in his rookie year and becoming a cultural phenomenon.
Allen Iverson won his only MVP award in 2001 after averaging 31.1 points per game, then dragged the 76ers past the Milwaukee Bucks and into the Finals almost entirely on his own.
Joel Embiid arrived in 2016 and became one of the most dominant offensive centers in NBA history, winning the scoring title in 2021-22 and earning the MVP award in 2022-23.
Joel Embiid became the first center to win the MVP award since Shaquille O'Neal in 2000, averaging 33.1 points per game in 2022-23 and winning the scoring title for a second consecutive season.
Gail Goodrich was one of the first great scorers in Suns history, providing perimeter scoring and setting the standard for what a shooting guard could be for the young franchise.
Paul Westphal averaged 25.2 points and 6.3 assists per game for the Suns in 1977-78, earning four consecutive All-Star selections and being one of the most explosive guards in the Western Conference.
Walter Davis was a six-time All-Star for Phoenix, earning the nickname The Greyhound for his smooth scoring style and averaging over 20 points per game throughout the decade.
Charles Barkley won the MVP award with the Phoenix Suns in 1993, averaging 25.6 points and 12.2 rebounds per game and leading the franchise to its first Finals appearance.
Steve Nash won back-to-back MVP awards in 2005 and 2006 while running the Seven Seconds or Less offense, revolutionizing the pace-and-space style that now defines modern NBA basketball.
Devin Booker scored 70 points against the Boston Celtics in March 2017 at just 20 years old, becoming the youngest player in NBA history to score 70 points in a single game.
Devin Booker led the Suns to the best record in the NBA in 2021-22, finishing second in MVP voting and earning his first All-Star selection while establishing himself as a franchise cornerstone.
Bill Walton won the MVP award and the Finals MVP in 1977, averaging 18.5 points, 13.4 rebounds, and 3.3 blocks per game to lead the underdog Trail Blazers to their only NBA championship.
Clyde Drexler was the engine of the Portland offense throughout the 1980s, earning five All-Star selections and establishing himself as one of the most complete guards of his era.
Clyde Drexler led the Blazers to the 1992 Finals, averaging 25.0 points per game in the postseason and engaging in one of the great individual Finals duels against Michael Jordan.
Rasheed Wallace was one of the most skilled big men of his era during his Portland tenure, combining post scoring, perimeter shooting, and explosive energy in a uniquely versatile package.
Damian Lillard became one of the most clutch players in NBA history, hitting legendary buzzer-beaters including a series-ending 37-foot three-pointer against the Oklahoma City Thunder in 2019.
Anfernee Simons emerged as Portland's leading scorer after Damian Lillard's trade, averaging a career-high 24.6 points per game in 2023-24 as the franchise began its next chapter.
Bobby Davies was a creative playmaker for the Rochester Royals, known as the Houdini of the Hardwood for his dribbling wizardry.
Bob Davies continued leading the Rochester Royals with his innovative style through the early 1950s, helping establish the franchise as one of the most respected in the early NBA.

Oscar Robertson averaged a triple-double for an entire season for the Cincinnati Royals in 1961-62, posting 30.8 points, 12.5 rebounds, and 11.4 assists per game in the most complete season in history.

Nate Archibald won both the scoring and assists titles in the same season for the Kansas City-Omaha Kings in 1972-73, the only player in NBA history to lead the league in both categories simultaneously.
Reggie Theus was a flashy, high-scoring guard for the Kansas City and Sacramento Kings, earning two All-Star selections and becoming the face of the relocated franchise throughout the decade.
Mitch Richmond won the 1995 All-Star Game MVP and earned six straight All-Star selections for Sacramento, averaging over 22 points per game across his Kings career.

Chris Webber was a four-time All-Star for Sacramento, averaging 24.5 points and 10.3 rebounds per game and leading the Kings' beautiful motion-based offense that many consider the most entertaining team of its era.
DeMarcus Cousins twice averaged over 27 points and 11 rebounds per game for Sacramento, establishing himself as one of the most dominant centers in the league despite playing on non-playoff teams.
De'Aaron Fox led Sacramento to its first playoff appearance in 17 years in 2023, averaging 25.2 points and 6.1 assists per game and winning the Play-In Tournament MVP award.

George Gervin joined the Spurs from the ABA and immediately became one of the most prolific scorers in NBA history, winning the scoring title in his first four eligible seasons with his silky finger-roll.
George Gervin continued his scoring dominance into the 1980s, averaging over 26 points per game and earning multiple All-Star selections as the Iceman remained one of the game's elite scorers.
David Robinson won the MVP award in 1995 after averaging 27.6 points, 10.8 rebounds, and 3.2 blocks per game, then teamed with Tim Duncan to win the 1999 championship as the Twin Towers.
Tim Duncan won four championships, three Finals MVPs, and two regular-season MVPs in the 2000s, anchoring one of the most consistent dynasties in North American professional sports history.
Kawhi Leonard won the 2014 Finals MVP after holding LeBron James to 19.3 points on 38.5% shooting, then won the Defensive Player of the Year award in both 2015 and 2016.

Victor Wembanyama won Rookie of the Year in 2024 after averaging 21.4 points, 10.6 rebounds, and 3.6 blocks per game, becoming the first rookie to average those numbers since Kareem Abdul-Jabbar in 1970.

Vince Carter won the Slam Dunk Contest in 2000 and was already electrifying audiences with his otherworldly athleticism after arriving in 1998, quickly becoming one of the most popular players in the world.
Vince Carter averaged 27.6 points per game in 2000-01 and single-handedly made the Raptors appointment television, earning the nickname Half-Man Half-Amazing for his dunks.

Kawhi Leonard hit the most famous shot in Raptors history in Game 7 of the 2019 Eastern Conference Semifinals, a four-bounce buzzer-beater over the Philadelphia 76ers that sent Toronto to its first Finals.
Pascal Siakam won the Most Improved Player award in 2019 and became a two-time All-Star, anchoring Toronto's rebuild as the first homegrown Raptors player to become a clear franchise centerpiece.
Pete Maravich was a dazzling showman for the New Orleans Jazz, averaging 31.1 points per game in 1976-77 and being one of the most purely entertaining scorers the league had ever produced.
Adrian Dantley was one of the most unstoppable scorers of the decade, winning two scoring titles and averaging 30.7 points per game in 1983-84 with his masterful post game for the Jazz.
John Stockton set the all-time assists record at 15,806 during the 1990s and paired with Karl Malone to run the pick-and-roll better than anyone in NBA history, making the Jazz a Finals contender.
Carlos Boozer averaged 20.9 points and 11.7 rebounds per game for the Jazz in 2007-08, earning two All-Star selections and being a key piece of Utah's conference finalist team.
Rudy Gobert won three Defensive Player of the Year awards as a Jazz player, revolutionizing the center position with his rim protection and pick-and-roll coverage.
Donovan Mitchell averaged 25.9 points per game for the Jazz, earning three All-Star selections and becoming one of the most beloved players in franchise history before being traded to Cleveland.
Walt Bellamy averaged 31.6 points and 19.0 rebounds per game as a rookie for the Chicago Packers in 1961-62, one of the greatest statistical rookie seasons in NBA history.

Elvin Hayes averaged over 20 points and 14 rebounds per game for the Washington Bullets throughout the 1970s, forming one of the era's most dominant frontcourts alongside Wes Unseld and winning the 1978 championship.

Jeff Ruland averaged 20.0 points and 13.0 rebounds per game in his healthy seasons with Washington, establishing himself as one of the most effective centers in the league before injuries derailed his career.
Chris Webber averaged 24.1 points and 10.3 rebounds per game in his Bullets debut, immediately becoming one of the most exciting power forwards in the league after being traded from Golden State.
Gilbert Arenas averaged 29.3 points per game in 2005-06 and hit so many clutch shots that the shot clock buzzer became known as Agent Zero time in Washington.
John Wall averaged 10.3 assists per game in 2016-17 and was one of the most explosive point guards in the Eastern Conference, earning five All-Star selections during his time with the Wizards.
Kyle Kuzma averaged a career-high 21.2 points per game in 2022-23, establishing himself as one of the better scorers in the Eastern Conference while leading a Wizards team in full rebuild mode.
